content.full_text:
City of Rocklin
3970 Rocklin Road
P.O. Box 1138
Rocklin, CA 95677
(916) 632-4000

January 20, 1992

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95812-0807

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

I am the Mayor of the City of Rocklin. This is a request for formal advice concerning my participation in City Council review and action on a proposed subdivision project located near my residence in the City of Rocklin.

The project, known as The Summit, is proposed for the top of a hill located behind my home within the City of Rocklin. The developable portion of the project site is a 300 to 800 foot wide hilltop rising 120 feet above the surrounding neighborhoods. The hill runs essentially north/south. There is existing residential development surrounding the hill on three sides: west, south, and east.

The site is currently zoned RE-20. The development request for a subdivision map and use permit is consistent with the current zoning. A rezoning to PD-RE-20 is being requested for technical reasons but does not change the density allowed on the site. The RE-20 zone is a low density residential zone, requiring minimum 20,000 square foot lots.

Virtually the entire west side of the hill has a slope approximating 30 percent, and is not proposed for development. (The City of Rocklin General Plan contains a policy prohibiting development on hillsides with a greater than 20% slope.) This slope is also heavily wooded. Access to the hilltop, as well as all utility hookups, are planned for the east side of the hill. The reasons for the design include the steepness of the slope and the number of trees on the west side, and the fact that the existing infrastructure on the east side of the hill was designed to service the hilltop: a street in an adjacent subdivision stubs into the logical access to the hilltop, and

Administrative Services

& Facilities
632-4100
FAX 632-4111

Engineering Department
632-4024
FAX 627-4759

Fire Department
632-4150
FAX 624-2677

Police Department
632-4060
FAX 624-2677

Public Works Department
632-4130
FAX 632-4173

Letter to Scott Hallabrin
Fair Political Practices Commission
January 20, 1992
Page 2

utilities in the subdivisions adjacent to the site on the
east were designed to extend up to the hilltop.  As a
result, there will be no development impact from the
development project extending down the western slope.

While not proposed, it is possible that a secondary access
could be designed that would bring traffic down the
northwest side and along Clover Valley Road (see map).
Evidence shows, however, that even if this were done, the
increase in traffic on Clover Valley Road would be nominal.

My home, which I own, backs up to the bottom of the hillside
on the west, abutting the project boundary.  The entrance to
the project is 1.1 road miles from my property.  From my
home, one cannot see the top of the ridge or any of the
developable area.  The distance between my property line and
the developable portion of the project is more than 300
feet.  Despite the common boundary, therefore, my property
is both physically and visually cutoff from the proposed
development.

Attached are maps showing the project site, the location of
my home, and other characteristics of the project.

It is hard to imagine any impact, either positive or
negative, of the development on me or my home.  Because of
existing oak trees in the open space area, the slope of the
hill, and the fact that virtually all traffic and services
will access the site from the east side of the hill, I am
unable to see how any decision I may make on the project
could affect my interest in my home.

As a part of the investigation of whether I would be
disqualified from acting on this matter, the City retained a
MAI real estate appraiser to give an opinion on what effect
on value, if any, development on the hilltop would have on
the homes in the surrounding neighborhoods, including the
west side.  He concluded it would have no effect whatsoever
on the homes in the neighborhood where I live.

Under California Code of Regulations §18702.3, the effect of
a decision on this project would be deemed material as to my
ownership interest in my home unless the decision would have

Letter to Scott Hallabrin
Fair Political Practices Commission
January 20, 1992
Page 3

absolutely no financial effect on my interest.  This rule
applies because my home is within 300 feet of the project
boundary.

Based on these facts, my questions are as follows:

1.  What criteria or evidence should a public official use
to determine whether or not a decision on a development
project will have a financial effect on that official's
ownership interest in real property?

2.  Should the slope area located between my home and the
developable portion of the project site be counted as
part of the development project for purposes of
applying §18702.3?

3.  If so, is it reasonably foreseeable that the decision
on this subdivision project will have a material
financial effect on my interest in my home,
distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, under Government Code §§87100 and 87103 and
California Code of Regulations §18702.3?

I understand that the normal turnaround time on requests for
formal advice is 21 working days.  This project is scheduled
to begin hearings before the City Planning Commission on
January 23, 1992, with hearings before the City Council to
follow soon thereafter.  I ask that you give this your
attention and render the advice as soon as possible.

If you need any further information in researching and
evaluating this question, or if you have any other questions
of me, you can reach me during the day at 457-2264.

Thank you.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Peter Hill
Mayor

Attach.

sections.question:
1. What criteria or evidence should a public official use to determine whether or not a decision on a development project will have a financial effect on that official's ownership interest in real property?

2. Should the slope area located between my home and the developable portion of the project site be counted as part of the development project for purposes of applying §18702.3?

3. If so, is it reasonably foreseeable that the decision on this subdivision project will have a material financial effect on my interest in my home, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, under Government Code §§87100 and 87103 and California Code of Regulations §18702.3?
sections.question_synthetic: The Mayor of Rocklin seeks advice on whether he must recuse himself from voting on a proposed subdivision project located near his home. Specifically, he asks what criteria determine whether a development decision will have a material financial effect on his property ownership interest, whether slope areas should be included in proximity calculations, and whether the project will foreseeably affect his home's value.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from a public official to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No formal conclusion section present. Document contains detailed factual background about the project and requestor's property. Date is January 20, 1992, with urgent timeline noted (hearings scheduled for January 23, 1992).

2. Should the slope area located between my home and the developable portion of the project site be counted as part of the development project for purposes of applying §18702.3?

3. If so, is it reasonably foreseeable that the decision on this subdivision project will have a material financial effect on my interest in my home, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, under Government Code §§87100 and 87103 and California Code of Regulations §18702.3?

embedding.summary: The Mayor of Rocklin requests formal FPPC advice regarding his potential conflict of interest in voting on The Summit subdivision project proposed for a hilltop behind his residence. He argues that despite owning property adjacent to the project boundary, physical separation, terrain barriers, and an appraiser's opinion showing no property value impact suggest he should not be required to disqualify himself.
